Are all financial businesses fundamentally playing a zero sum game, or are there ways to achieve a positive sum system?
It seems to me that any aspect of our financial system that results in the creation of value by providing liquidity is playing a positive sum game.

But are the parts of the financial system engaging in speculative investing playing a positive sum game? Is a hedge fund able to create value? Or do hedge funds simply take advantage of the meta patterns in finance? Picking up the crumbs if you will.
More succinctly: what is the ratio on Wall Street between rent-seekers and financial creators/innovators?
Why do we have to get meal kits in the mail? Wouldn’t it make sense for a grocery store to sell meal kits?

Currently working on my first real web app in a long time. As much as people complain about the current state of frontend dev(I agree), it’s pretty incredible that you can create an entire app from just JSX if you use react and tailwind.

@DanielLockyer @bernoussama @hard_simp This is a fundamental failing of modern AI. We trust you to merge because there’s an understanding that if you mess up enough, you’ll get fired. You are incentivized to only merge code you know works. The ai doesn’t care about preserving itself and cannot double check itself.

@bernoussama @DanielLockyer @hard_simp The AI as a tie breaker comes back to the fundamental problem. How can we trust the tie breaker? Who is to say the tie breaker is more correct than the agents? If it’s smarter and more aligned than the agents, why don’t we just ditch the agents and have only the tie breakers?

Day 2 of Scaling Law Optimizing Myself:

Yesterday I started listening to podcasts at 2x and using spreeder to read at 500 wpm.

Today I continued reading at that rate. Felt a lot more comfortable and was way less overwhelming.

The overwhelming feeling is likely due to the feeling of lack of control. Podcasts at 2x and spreeder moves on without you. When I miss something, it’s a pain in the butt to go back, so I don’t go back.

I likely feel less overwhelmed because I got better.

I’m concluding my “human scaling law” experiment.

- Speed reading isn’t useful. High density content necessarily requires a slower and more thoughtful approach.
- Podcasts can be sped up 1.5-2.0x with no consequence.
- Increase in content requires an increase in synthesis.

It's quite remarkable how slow Python is. It's almost like they were trying to make the slowest language possible. 

The messed up part is that the speed isn't even the worst aspect of Python. It's that people genuinely try to use it in production scenarios.
And don't even start with me with the whole "python is only slow if you use it wrong". Like bro, surely you realize numpy is one library in a sea of shitty ass slow libraries.
Python is a tool. It's great for scripting. It's like bash. Great in specific scenarios, terrible in many.
I'm saying this as someone deeply familiar with Python. I use it in my day job.
Saying all that, Python is still super cool. Jupyter / matplotlib / pandas is a fun combo.

https://t.co/uBxOhBKaBT

Really great article outlining "what's wrong with AMD". Super thorough. Here's the executive recommendations to AMD(TL;DR):

1. Give AMD Engineers more compute and engineering resources to fix and improve the AMD ecosystem, they have very few internal gpu boxes relative to what Nvidia provides to their engineers. Tensorwave, the largest AMD GPU Cloud has given GPU time for free to a team at AMD to fix software issues, which is insane given they paid for the GPUs.

2. AMD needs to hook up thousands more of MI300X, MI325X to PyTorch CI/CD for automated testing to ensure there is no AMD performance regressions & functional AMD bugs. Nvidia has given thousands of GPUs for PyTorch CI/CD to ensure an amazing out of box experience

3. The AMD Executive Team should personally and intensively internally test (i.e., “dogfood”) products that are being shipped to the public rather than focus on testing internal builds. Preferably dogfood during livestream to show the authentic out of box experience. This is like how @realGeorgeHotz  livestreams.

4. AMD should collaborate with Meta to get production LLM training workloads working as soon as possible on PyTorch ROCm, AMD’s answer to CUDA, as commonly, PyTorch code paths that Meta isn’t using have numerous bugs.

5. Move away from over-reliance on properly setting numerous environment flags (up to dozens) to make an AMD deployment usable. Instead, bake these settings into the default configuration. Make the out of the box experience usable!

6. Focus on making out of box experience good instead of over-reliance on custom VIP images that build all dependencies from source code main@specificcommit and take 5 hours to build.

7. Stop expecting end users to use PYTORCH_TUNABLE_OPS which is a prototype buggy feature and is not respectful of the end users time as it takes ~1 hour for the end user to tune every time an end user wants to make any changes to their code.

8. AMD should submit MLPerf Training GPT-3 175B results. MLPerf is an apples-to-apples benchmarking methodology that uses time to convergence as the north star.

9. We want AMD to be competitive and are open to meet with more detailed feedback on how to fix the AMD Datacenter GPU Ecosystem for the better.

The more I learn the more I realize Jobs was right:

“Good artists copy, great artists steal”
Just take The Man Who Sold the World by Bowie/Nirvana. The Bowie version is great, but Nirvana took it to another level.

IP protections for art are antiquated. Patents are to reward investments in RnD. There’s no equivalent for art. The marginal cost of art is 0 or close to 0. Once you make it one time, you’ve made it a million.

Software realized this a long time ago. You don’t make money on the software, you make money *around* the software. Same thing for art. 

Unfortunately, record labels have a death grip on the industry. Hopefully that changes.
Steal all the IP, and let the lawyers sort it out.

Is fomo counterproductive in the modern world?
 
This instinct might be productive in hunter gatherer scenarios. Your successful peers may have good survival info.

But in more complex scenarios where survival is not in question, it serves as a distraction. It is better to stick…

Is fomo counterproductive in the modern world?
 
This instinct might be productive in hunter gatherer scenarios. Your successful peers may have good survival info.

But in more complex scenarios where survival is not in question, it serves as a distraction. It is better to stick to a plan until a hypothesis can be validated, than following the locally confirmed hypothesis of another.

Ted Chiang on why sci-fi is the best medium to explore philosophical questions:

When a philosophical thought experiment is supposed to be set in the actual world, the situation often has a contrived quality. For example, the famous “trolley problem” is supposedly set in the actual world, but it describes a situation that is extremely artificial; in the real world, we have safeguards precisely to avoid situations like that.
